title: Tissues Vocabulary
description: Essential terminology for epithelial, connective, muscle, and nervous tissue

questions.q: A tissue with cells arranged in a single layer is classified as:
questions.explain: Simple epithelium has one layer of cells — all touching the basement membrane. It's thin, allowing diffusion and filtration.

optionExplains.text: Stratified means multiple layers — cells stacked on cells.
Simple — one layer where every cell contacts the basement membrane. Found where absorption or filtration occurs: Lungs, blood vessels, intestinal lining.
Pseudostratified appears layered but isn't — all cells touch the basement membrane.
Transitional is a special stratified type that stretches — found in bladder.

questions.q: Squamous cells are described as:
questions.explain: Squamous means flat and scale-like — these cells are wider than they are tall, like floor tiles or fish scales.

optionExplains.text: Cube-shaped cells are cuboidal — equal dimensions.
Column-shaped cells are columnar — taller than wide.
Squamous — flat like scales. The word comes from Latin 'squama' meaning scale. They line surfaces where thinness matters: Air sacs, blood vessels.
Irregular shapes describe some connective tissue cells, not squamous epithelium.

questions.q: The basement membrane is located:
questions.explain: The basement membrane anchors epithelium to underlying connective tissue. It's a thin layer of extracellular matrix at the basal surface.

optionExplains.text: Apical surface faces the exterior or cavity — opposite of where basement membrane sits.
The basement membrane separates epithelium from connective tissue beneath. It provides structural support and filters substances passing between tissues.
Nothing called basement membrane is inside the nucleus.
Muscle fibers have sarcolemma and endomysium, not basement membrane in the epithelial sense.

questions.q: Cilia are found on the apical surface of which tissue type?
questions.explain: Pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium lines respiratory passages. Cilia beat to move mucus and trapped particles upward.

optionExplains.text: Simple squamous is too thin for cilia — it specializes in diffusion.
Pseudostratified ciliated columnar — the respiratory epithelium. Cilia wave in coordinated sweeps, pushing mucus (and debris) toward the throat.
Stratified squamous protects against abrasion — no cilia needed.
Adipose is connective tissue storing fat. No cilia.

questions.q: Goblet cells produce:
questions.explain: Goblet cells are unicellular glands that secrete mucus — a protective, lubricating glycoprotein coating.

optionExplains.text: Keratin is produced by keratinocytes in skin. Different cell type.
Mucus — goblet cells are shaped like wine goblets filled with mucin granules. They're scattered among columnar cells in respiratory and digestive linings.
Collagen is made by fibroblasts in connective tissue.
Histamine comes from mast cells and basophils.

questions.q: The most abundant protein in the body is:
questions.explain: Collagen makes up about 25-30% of total body protein. It forms the structural framework of connective tissues.

optionExplains.text: Elastin provides stretch but is far less abundant than collagen.
Keratin is abundant in skin and hair but not the most abundant overall.
Collagen — the body's structural scaffolding. It forms tendons, ligaments, bone matrix, dermis, fascia. Triple-helix structure gives incredible tensile strength.
Actin is abundant in muscle but doesn't exceed collagen body-wide.

questions.q: Fibroblasts are the primary cells of:
questions.explain: Fibroblasts produce and maintain the extracellular matrix of connective tissue — they secrete collagen, elastin, and ground substance.

optionExplains.text: Epithelial cells are different — they form surface coverings.
Connective tissue proper — fibroblasts are the master builders. They spin out collagen fibers and maintain the matrix. When you heal a wound, fibroblasts rush in.
Muscle tissue has myocytes (muscle cells), not fibroblasts.
Nervous tissue has neurons and glial cells.

questions.q: Dense regular connective tissue is found in:
questions.explain: Dense regular connective tissue has collagen fibers aligned in parallel — perfectly suited for tendons and ligaments that resist pull in one direction.

optionExplains.text: Dermis contains dense IRREGULAR connective tissue — fibers in many directions.
Tendons and ligaments — parallel collagen bundles resist unidirectional stress. Tendons connect muscle to bone; ligaments connect bone to bone.
Around organs is loose connective tissue, allowing movement.
Bones are osseous tissue — mineralized matrix, not dense regular.

questions.q: Adipose tissue functions primarily to:
questions.explain: Adipose tissue stores triglycerides for energy reserve and insulates the body against heat loss. It also cushions and protects organs.

optionExplains.text: Nerve impulse transmission is nervous tissue function.
Store energy and insulate — adipocytes pack triglycerides. Subcutaneous fat insulates; visceral fat cushions organs. It's also an endocrine organ secreting leptin.
Red blood cell production is red bone marrow.
Contraction is muscle tissue function.

questions.q: Cartilage cells are called:
questions.explain: Chondrocytes are cartilage cells — they live in lacunae within the cartilage matrix they produced.

optionExplains.text: Osteocytes are mature bone cells.
Chondrocytes — 'chondro' means cartilage. These cells sit in lacunae, surrounded by the rubbery matrix they secreted as chondroblasts.
Adipocytes are fat cells.
Fibroblasts are general connective tissue cells, not specific to cartilage.

questions.q: The type of cartilage found in intervertebral discs is:
questions.explain: Fibrocartilage contains thick collagen bundles making it tough and compressible — perfect for intervertebral discs, knee menisci, and pubic symphysis.

optionExplains.text: Hyaline cartilage covers joint surfaces but lacks the strength for disc compression.
Elastic cartilage is flexible (ear, epiglottis) but not strong enough for spine.
Fibrocartilage — dense collagen fibers withstand compression and tension. Intervertebral discs absorb spinal shock; their fibrocartilage annulus surrounds the gel nucleus.
Intervertebral discs are cartilage, not bone.

questions.q: Endocrine glands release their secretions into:
questions.explain: Endocrine glands are ductless — they secrete hormones directly into the bloodstream for distribution throughout the body.

optionExplains.text: That's exocrine glands — they use ducts (sweat glands, salivary glands).
Bloodstream — endocrine glands release hormones into surrounding capillaries. The blood carries hormones to target cells anywhere in the body.
Body cavities receive serous secretions, not typically endocrine hormones.
Some hormones enter lymph, but blood is the primary distribution route.

questions.q: Exocrine glands that release whole cells as their secretion are classified as:
questions.explain: Holocrine secretion involves the entire cell rupturing and releasing its contents. Sebaceous glands are holocrine.

optionExplains.text: Merocrine secretion releases only the product via exocytosis — no cell destruction.
Apocrine secretion pinches off the cell apex with the product.
Holocrine — the whole cell becomes the secretion. Sebaceous gland cells fill with oils, die, and rupture. New cells replace them continuously.
Endocrine glands secrete into blood, not via duct secretion modes.

questions.q: The ground substance of connective tissue is composed primarily of:
questions.explain: Ground substance is the gel-like material between cells and fibers — made of proteoglycans, glycosaminoglycans, and adhesive glycoproteins.

optionExplains.text: Collagen fibers are embedded IN ground substance but aren't the substance itself.
Proteoglycans and glycosaminoglycans (like hyaluronic acid) trap water, creating a hydrated gel. This allows diffusion of nutrients and provides resilience.
Calcium salts are in bone matrix, not general ground substance.
Keratin is a structural protein in epithelium, not connective tissue matrix.

questions.q: Elastic connective tissue is abundant in:
questions.explain: Elastic connective tissue — rich in elastin fibers — allows structures to stretch and recoil. Large arteries need this to handle blood pressure pulses.

optionExplains.text: Tendons are dense regular with collagen — they resist stretch, not accommodate it.
Large artery walls — elastin lets them expand with systole and recoil in diastole. This maintains blood pressure between heartbeats. The aorta is highly elastic.
Bone is rigid with mineralized matrix — no elastin prominence.
Hyaline cartilage has some elasticity but isn't classified as elastic connective tissue.

questions.q: Osteocytes communicate through cell processes in channels called:
questions.explain: Canaliculi are tiny tunnels radiating from lacunae, containing osteocyte processes. They allow nutrient exchange and cell-to-cell communication.

optionExplains.text: Haversian canals carry blood vessels through bone — too large for cell processes.
Volkmann's canals connect Haversian canals — vascular channels, not for cell processes.
Canaliculi — tiny canals containing osteocyte extensions. Gap junctions connect neighboring processes, allowing ions and small molecules to pass through bone matrix.
Lacunae are the spaces where osteocytes live — canaliculi radiate from them.

questions.q: The tissue that lines the urinary bladder and can stretch is:
questions.explain: Transitional epithelium (urothelium) is specialized stratified tissue that stretches as the bladder fills. Cells change shape from dome to flat.

optionExplains.text: Simple squamous is one thin layer — can't handle bladder distension.
Stratified squamous protects but doesn't stretch like transitional.
Transitional epithelium — uniquely stretchable. When empty, cells appear dome-shaped and layered; when full, they flatten. Found in bladder, ureters, urethra.
Simple columnar is for secretion/absorption — not stretching.

questions.q: Mast cells release which substance during allergic reactions?
questions.explain: Mast cells contain granules packed with histamine. When triggered by allergens, they degranulate — releasing histamine that causes inflammation.

optionExplains.text: Collagen is made by fibroblasts, not mast cells.
Histamine — mast cell granules burst when IgE antibodies bind allergens. Histamine causes vasodilation, increased permeability, and itching. Antihistamines block its effects.
Melanin is produced by melanocytes in skin.
Mucus comes from goblet cells, not mast cells.

questions.q: Reticular connective tissue forms the framework of:
questions.explain: Reticular tissue — a network of thin reticular fibers — provides the structural scaffolding for lymphoid organs where immune cells reside.

optionExplains.text: Tendons are dense regular connective tissue with collagen bundles.
Lymph nodes, spleen, and bone marrow use reticular fiber networks. These delicate meshworks support and filter immune cells moving through.
Joint capsules are dense irregular connective tissue.
Dermis is dense irregular — collagen-rich for strength.

questions.q: The apical surface of epithelium faces:
questions.explain: Apical means top — the apical surface faces outward toward an external environment, body cavity, or lumen of a tube.

optionExplains.text: Basement membrane is at the basal (bottom) surface — opposite of apical.
Apical surface contacts the exterior or lumen. In skin, it faces outside; in intestine, it faces the gut lumen. This is where microvilli and cilia project.
Blood vessels are in connective tissue below epithelium.
That's the basal surface, which rests on connective tissue via basement membrane.

questions.q: Keratinized stratified squamous epithelium is found in:
questions.explain: Skin epidermis is keratinized — surface cells fill with keratin protein and die, forming a tough, waterproof barrier.

optionExplains.text: Mouth and esophagus have NON-keratinized stratified squamous — moist environments.
Epidermis — keratinocytes fill with keratin as they migrate upward, then die. This creates the dead, protective stratum corneum that resists abrasion and water loss.
Blood vessels have simple squamous endothelium — no keratin.
Alveoli have simple squamous for gas exchange — paper thin.

questions.q: Lacunae in cartilage and bone are:
questions.explain: Lacunae are small cavities in the matrix where chondrocytes (cartilage) or osteocytes (bone) reside.

optionExplains.text: Blood vessel channels in bone are Haversian and Volkmann's canals.
Lacunae — Latin for 'little lakes.' Each lacuna houses one cell that originally secreted the surrounding matrix. The cell is trapped in its own product.
Protein fibers (collagen, elastin) are in the matrix between lacunae.
Mineral deposits are in bone matrix but lacunae are cell-containing spaces.

questions.q: Simple cuboidal epithelium is found in:
questions.explain: Simple cuboidal epithelium lines kidney tubules, where cells actively transport substances. The shape balances surface area with secretory capacity.

optionExplains.text: Skin surface is stratified squamous — multiple layers for protection.
Kidney tubules — simple cuboidal cells reabsorb water, glucose, and ions. Also found in small gland ducts and ovary surface.
Blood vessels have simple squamous endothelium.
Respiratory bronchioles transition from ciliated columnar to cuboidal, but simple cuboidal is characteristic of kidney tubules.

questions.q: Blood is classified as which type of connective tissue?
questions.explain: Blood is fluid connective tissue — cells suspended in liquid matrix (plasma). It transports substances and provides immune defense.

optionExplains.text: Dense connective tissue has abundant fibers — blood has dissolved proteins instead.
Loose connective tissue has gel-like ground substance — blood has liquid plasma.
Fluid connective tissue — blood and lymph. The matrix is liquid plasma; cells float within it. Despite being liquid, blood fits connective tissue criteria.
Supporting connective tissue refers to cartilage and bone — solid matrices.

questions.q: Tissue repair that replaces damaged tissue with scar tissue is called:
questions.explain: Fibrosis replaces functional tissue with fibrous scar tissue. It restores structural integrity but not original function.

optionExplains.text: Regeneration replaces damaged cells with SAME type — full functional recovery.
Fibrosis — when regeneration isn't possible, fibroblasts produce collagen scar tissue. Hearts can't regenerate muscle, so infarcts heal by fibrosis.
Hyperplasia is increase in cell NUMBER, not scar formation.
Metaplasia is one mature cell type changing to another — not scar formation.
